title: Partial Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999): Fourth Grade Evidence
text:
How much does class size actually affect student learning? This seemingly simple question has proven surprisingly hard to answer. The core problem is endogeneity: schools often assign struggling students to smaller classes, and wealthier districts tend to have both larger schools and better outcomes. A naive comparison of large vs. small classes conflates the effect of class size with these confounding factors.
Angrist and Lavy (1999) solve this identification problem using an elegant natural experiment rooted in a 12th-century rabbinical rule. Maimonides’ Rule, still enforced in Israeli public schools today, caps class size at 40 students. When a grade’s enrollment hits 41, the cohort must be split into two classes; at 81, into three; and so on. This creates sharp, predictable discontinuities in class size as a function of enrollment — discontinuities that have nothing to do with student ability or family background.
The key insight is that a school with 40 students in a grade will have one class of 40, while a school with 41 students will have two classes of about 20. These two schools are likely very similar in every other way, yet experience a dramatic difference in class size driven purely by an administrative rule. Angrist and Lavy use the predicted class size from Maimonides’ Rule as an instrumental variable, allowing them to isolate the causal effect of class size on test scores.
Formally, the predicted class size (instrument) is:
\[f_{sc} = \frac{e_s}{\lfloor (e_s - 1)/40 \rfloor + 1}\]
where \(e_s\) is the beginning-of-year grade enrollment in school \(s\). This paper is a landmark application of the regression-discontinuity (RDD) design in economics.

title: Partial Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999): Fourth Grade Evidence
text:
Main Analysis

Data
The data cover all Jewish public elementary schools in Israel tested in 1991. The unit of observation is the class. Outcomes are average reading (verbal) and math composite scores (scaled 1–100). The key control variable is tipuach, the percent of students from disadvantaged backgrounds (the PD index).

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from IPython.display import display

# Load 4th grade data
df = pd.read_stata("files/final4.dta")

# Fix scores > 100 (coding artifact in original data)
df["avgverb"] = np.where(df["avgverb"] > 100, df["avgverb"] - 100, df["avgverb"])
df["avgmath"] = np.where(df["avgmath"] > 100, df["avgmath"] - 100, df["avgmath"])

# Maimonides' Rule predicted class size
df["func1"] = df["c_size"] / (np.floor((df["c_size"] - 1) / 40) + 1)

# Set scores to missing if no test takers
df.loc[df["verbsize"] == 0, "avgverb"] = np.nan
df.loc[df["mathsize"] == 0, "avgmath"] = np.nan

# Apply sample restrictions (identical to original Stata do-file)
df = df[(df["classize"] > 1) & (df["classize"] < 45) & (df["c_size"] > 5)]
df = df[(df["c_leom"] == 1) & (df["c_pik"] < 3)]

print(f"Sample size: {len(df):,} classes")
print(f"Mean verbal score: {df['avgverb'].mean():.1f}")
print(f"Mean math score:   {df['avgmath'].mean():.1f}")
print(f"Mean class size:   {df['classize'].mean():.1f}")

Sample size: 2,053 classes
Mean verbal score: 72.5
Mean math score:   68.9
Mean class size:   30.3




Figure 1b — Class Size by Enrollment (4th Grade)
This figure replicates Figure 1b from the paper. It plots actual average class size and the Maimonides’ Rule predicted class size against school enrollment. The sawtooth pattern in the dashed line (the rule) and the corresponding drops in actual class size at enrollment multiples of 40 are clearly visible — these discontinuities are the source of identifying variation.

fig_data = (
    df.groupby("c_size")
    .agg(actual=("classize", "mean"), predicted=("func1", "mean"))
    .reset_index()
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

ax.plot(fig_data["c_size"], fig_data["actual"],
        color="black", linewidth=0.9, label="Actual class size")
ax.plot(fig_data["c_size"], fig_data["predicted"],
        color="black", linewidth=0.9, linestyle="--", label="Maimonides Rule")

for y in [20, 25, 30, 35, 40]:
    ax.axhline(y, color="gray", linewidth=0.35, linestyle="--")

ax.set_xlabel("Enrollment count", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Class size", fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("Figure 1b. Fourth Grade", fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlim(0, 220)
ax.set_ylim(0, 45)
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(ticker.MultipleLocator(20))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








The actual class size closely tracks the Maimonides’ Rule prediction, with clear drops at enrollment = 40, 80, 120, and 160. This confirms the rule has real bite — it is a strong first stage for the IV strategy.


Table II (Columns 7 & 10) — Naive OLS
Before introducing the instrument, we estimate a simple OLS of test scores on actual class size with no controls. Standard errors are clustered by school (schlcode), following Moulton (1986).

def ols_cluster(outcome, regressors, data, cluster):
    cols = [outcome] + regressors + [cluster]
    d = data[cols].dropna()
    formula = outcome + " ~ " + " + ".join(regressors)
    m = smf.ols(formula, data=d).fit(
        cov_type="cluster", cov_kwds={"groups": d[cluster]}
    )
    return m

m_t2c7  = ols_cluster("avgverb", ["classize"], df, "schlcode")
m_t2c10 = ols_cluster("avgmath", ["classize"], df, "schlcode")

rows = []
for label, m, var in [
    ("Col 7: Verbal ~ Class Size", m_t2c7, "classize"),
    ("Col 10: Math ~ Class Size",  m_t2c10, "classize"),
]:
    rows.append({
        "Specification": label,
        "Class Size Coef": f"{m.params[var]:.3f}",
        "Std. Error":      f"({m.bse[var]:.3f})",
        "N":               int(m.nobs),
        "R²":              f"{m.rsquared:.3f}",
    })

t2 = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Specification")
display(t2)

Interpretation: Without controls, class size has a positive coefficient — larger classes appear to be associated with higher test scores. This is the selection bias at work: larger schools in Israel tend to be in wealthier urban areas with higher-scoring students. The raw correlation is misleading.


Table III (Panel A, Columns 7, 9, 11) — Reduced-Form RDD
Instead of regressing scores on actual class size, we now regress scores on the Maimonides’ Rule instrument (func1), controlling for PD (tipuach) and optionally enrollment (c_size). This is the reduced-form of the RDD strategy.

m_t3c7  = ols_cluster("avgverb", ["func1", "tipuach"],           df, "schlcode")
m_t3c9  = ols_cluster("avgverb", ["func1", "tipuach", "c_size"], df, "schlcode")
m_t3c11 = ols_cluster("avgmath", ["func1", "tipuach"],           df, "schlcode")

rows = []
for label, m, var in [
    ("Col 7:  Verbal ~ func1 + PD",                m_t3c7,  "func1"),
    ("Col 9:  Verbal ~ func1 + PD + Enrollment",   m_t3c9,  "func1"),
    ("Col 11: Math   ~ func1 + PD",                m_t3c11, "func1"),
]:
    rows.append({
        "Specification": label,
        "func1 Coef": f"{m.params[var]:.3f}",
        "Std. Error": f"({m.bse[var]:.3f})",
        "N":          int(m.nobs),
        "R²":         f"{m.rsquared:.3f}",
    })

t3 = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Specification")
display(t3)

Interpretation: Once we control for PD and use the exogenous variation from Maimonides’ Rule, the coefficient on func1 becomes negative for reading scores (−0.085 to −0.089). A larger predicted class size — driven solely by the administrative rule — is associated with lower test scores. The effect on math scores is near zero and insignificant for 4th graders. These point estimates match the paper exactly.
Note: Standard errors are clustered by school using HC cluster-robust methods. The original paper applies Moulton (1986) corrections, which may produce slightly different SEs; point estimates are identical.

title: Partial Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999): Fourth Grade Evidence
section: Something Additional — Why Does the Sign Flip? Visualizing Bias vs. Causation
text:
Something Additional — Why Does the Sign Flip? Visualizing Bias vs. Causation
The naive OLS and the reduced-form RDD tell completely opposite stories. This section makes that contrast concrete and explains why the RDD estimate is the credible one.

The Sign Flip in Numbers

comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "Method": ["Naive OLS (Table II)", "Naive OLS (Table II)",
                "Reduced-Form RDD (Table III)", "Reduced-Form RDD (Table III)"],
    "Outcome":    ["Verbal", "Math", "Verbal", "Math"],
    "Regressor":  ["Actual class size"] * 2 + ["Predicted class size (Maimonides)"] * 2,
    "Coefficient": [
        m_t2c7.params["classize"],
        m_t2c10.params["classize"],
        m_t3c7.params["func1"],
        m_t3c11.params["func1"],
    ],
    "Std. Error": [
        m_t2c7.bse["classize"],
        m_t2c10.bse["classize"],
        m_t3c7.bse["func1"],
        m_t3c11.bse["func1"],
    ],
})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))

colors = ["#c0392b", "#c0392b", "#2980b9", "#2980b9"]
markers = ["o", "s", "o", "s"]
labels_plot = ["OLS Verbal", "OLS Math", "RDD Verbal", "RDD Math"]
x_positions = [0, 1, 3, 4]

for i, (_, row) in enumerate(comparison.iterrows()):
    ax.errorbar(
        x_positions[i], row["Coefficient"],
        yerr=1.96 * row["Std. Error"],
        fmt=markers[i], color=colors[i], markersize=9,
        capsize=5, linewidth=1.8, label=labels_plot[i]
    )

ax.axhline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8, linestyle="-")
ax.set_xticks([0.5, 3.5])
ax.set_xticklabels(["Naive OLS\n(no controls)", "Reduced-Form RDD\n(+ PD control, Maimonides IV)"],
                   fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("Coefficient on class size measure", fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("Sign Reversal: Naive OLS vs. Reduced-Form RDD (4th Grade)", fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=9, loc="upper right")
ax.set_xlim(-1, 5.5)
ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










Why the RDD Estimate Is Credible
The sign reversal — from positive (OLS) to negative (RDD) — is not a mystery. It reflects two distinct sources of bias in the naive OLS:
1. Between-school selection bias. Larger schools in Israel are disproportionately located in prosperous urban areas. These schools also tend to have larger class sizes and higher-scoring students. This creates a spurious positive correlation between class size and test scores across schools.
2. Within-school selection bias. Even within the same school, principals may assign weaker students to smaller, remedial classes. This further inflates the OLS coefficient.
The Maimonides’ Rule instrument sidesteps both problems. The rule assigns predicted class size based purely on enrollment thresholds — whether a cohort falls just above or just below a multiple of 40. This threshold is effectively random with respect to student ability or family wealth. A school with 41 students is nearly identical to a school with 39 students in every way except that the former must split into two classes of ~20 while the latter has one class of 39.
By controlling for smooth functions of enrollment (the c_size covariate) and the PD index, and using only the discontinuous variation in func1 as the source of identification, the RDD isolates the causal channel: class size → test scores, with all confounders held fixed.
The result: a ten-student reduction in predicted class size is associated with roughly a 0.85–0.89 point increase in 4th grade reading scores — a credible, causally-identified estimate that the naive OLS would have hidden entirely.

title: Does Blueprinty’s Software Help Engineers Win More Patents?
text:
Blueprinty is a small software company that builds tools specifically for preparing and submitting patent applications to the US Patent and Trademark Office. Their marketing team wants to make a bold claim: engineering firms that use Blueprinty’s software are more likely to get their patents approved.
On the surface, this sounds like a question you could answer by simply comparing patent counts between customers and non-customers. But it isn’t that simple. Blueprinty’s customers are not chosen at random — firms that actively seek out specialized patent software are probably already more sophisticated, better resourced, or operating in regions with stronger innovation ecosystems. Any raw difference in patent counts between the two groups could easily reflect these pre-existing advantages rather than any effect of the software itself.
To study this more carefully, Blueprinty has shared data on 1,500 mature engineering firms, each with information on the number of patents they received over the last five years, their regional location, their age since incorporation, and whether they are a Blueprinty customer. Because the outcome is a count (non-negative integers), we model it using Poisson regression, estimated via Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). This lets us hold age and region constant while estimating the association between software use and patent counts — a more credible comparison than looking at raw group means alone.

title: Does Blueprinty’s Software Help Engineers Win More Patents?
section: A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood
text:
A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood

Why Poisson?
Patent counts are non-negative integers: 0, 1, 2, 3, … A Normal distribution is a poor fit because it places probability mass on negative values and treats the data as continuous. The Poisson distribution is the natural starting point for non-negative integer outcomes. Its single parameter λ controls both the mean and the variance of the distribution, and the PMF is:
\[
f(Y \mid \lambda) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^Y}{Y!}, \qquad Y = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
\]


Deriving the Log-Likelihood
Given \(n\) independent observations \(Y_1, \ldots, Y_n\), the joint likelihood is:
\[
L(\lambda \mid \mathbf{Y}) = \prod_{i=1}^n \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{Y_i}}{Y_i!}
\]
Taking the natural log turns the product into a sum (and makes optimization far easier numerically):
\[
\ell(\lambda \mid \mathbf{Y}) = \sum_{i=1}^n \left( -\lambda + Y_i \ln \lambda - \ln Y_i! \right)
\]
\[
= -n\lambda + \ln\lambda \sum_{i=1}^n Y_i - \sum_{i=1}^n \ln Y_i!
\]


Analytic MLE
Setting \(\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = 0\):
\[
\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = -n + \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i = 0 \implies \hat{\lambda}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{Y}
\]
This makes intuitive sense: the MLE for the Poisson rate is simply the sample mean, because \(\mathbb{E}[Y] = \lambda\) under the Poisson model.


Coding the Log-Likelihood


Code
from scipy.special import gammaln  # log(Y!) = log(Gamma(Y+1))

def poisson_loglikelihood(lam, Y):
    """Log-likelihood of Poisson(lambda) for observed counts Y."""
    if lam <= 0:
        return -np.inf
    return np.sum(-lam + Y * np.log(lam) - gammaln(Y + 1))

Y = df["patents"].values




Visualizing the Log-Likelihood


Code
lambdas = np.linspace(0.5, 10, 300)
ll_vals = [poisson_loglikelihood(l, Y) for l in lambdas]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))
ax.plot(lambdas, ll_vals, color=BLUE, linewidth=2)
ax.axvline(Y.mean(), color="crimson", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.8,
           label=f"$\\hat{{\\lambda}}_{{MLE}} = \\bar{{Y}} = {Y.mean():.3f}$")
ax.set_xlabel("λ (Poisson Rate Parameter)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Log-Likelihood ℓ(λ | Y)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("Poisson Log-Likelihood over the Observed Patent Data", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("loglik_curve.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()









The curve is concave and peaks exactly at \(\bar{Y}\) — visually confirming the analytic result. Values of λ far from the sample mean produce dramatically lower log-likelihoods, showing that the data are highly informative about the parameter.


Numerical MLE with scipy.optimize


Code
neg_ll = lambda lam: -poisson_loglikelihood(lam[0], Y)
result = optimize.minimize(neg_ll, x0=[3.0], method="Nelder-Mead")
lambda_mle = result.x[0]

print(f"Numerical MLE:  λ̂ = {lambda_mle:.5f}")
print(f"Sample mean:    Ȳ  = {Y.mean():.5f}")


Numerical MLE:  λ̂ = 3.68467
Sample mean:    Ȳ  = 3.68467


The numerical optimizer recovers the sample mean to five decimal places, exactly as the analytic derivation predicts. This cross-check confirms that the log-likelihood function is coded correctly before we move on to the regression setting.

title: Does Blueprinty’s Software Help Engineers Win More Patents?
text:
Poisson Regression

From a Fixed Rate to Firm-Specific Rates
The simple Poisson model assumed every firm shares the same patent rate λ. That’s clearly unrealistic — firms differ in age, location, and technology adoption. Poisson regression lets each firm have its own rate:
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i), \qquad \lambda_i = \exp(X_i'\beta)
\]
We use the exponential link because \(\lambda_i\) must be strictly positive, while the linear predictor \(X_i'\beta\) can be any real number. The exponential function maps \(\mathbb{R} \to (0, \infty)\), keeping predictions valid.


Updating the Log-Likelihood


Code
def poisson_regression_loglikelihood(beta, Y, X):
    """Poisson regression log-likelihood. lambda_i = exp(X_i @ beta)."""
    lam = np.exp(X @ beta)
    return np.sum(-lam + Y * np.log(lam) - gammaln(Y + 1))




Constructing the Design Matrix


Code
# One-hot encode region, drop one category to avoid multicollinearity
region_dummies = pd.get_dummies(df["region"], drop_first=True).astype(float)

X_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "intercept": 1.0,
    "age": df["age"],
    "age_sq": df["age"] ** 2,
}).join(region_dummies).join(df[["iscustomer"]].astype(float))

X = X_df.values
feature_names = X_df.columns.tolist()
print("Design matrix columns:", feature_names)
print("Shape:", X.shape)


Design matrix columns: ['intercept', 'age', 'age_sq', 'Northeast', 'Northwest', 'South', 'Southwest', 'iscustomer']
Shape: (1500, 8)


Design choices:

Intercept: always included as the first column.
Age + Age²: firm age likely has a non-linear relationship with patent output — very young and very old firms may differ from those in their prime. Including the squared term lets the model capture this curvature.
Region dummies: we include indicators for all but one region (the dropped reference category). Keeping all regions would create perfect collinearity with the intercept, making the system unsolvable.
iscustomer: the key variable of interest.



Finding the MLE with scipy.optimize


Code
from scipy.optimize import minimize
from scipy.linalg import inv

def neg_loglik(beta):
    eta = X @ beta
    mu = np.exp(eta)
    return -np.sum(Y * eta - mu - gammaln(Y + 1))

def neg_score(beta):
    """Gradient of the negative log-likelihood: X.T @ (mu - Y)"""
    mu = np.exp(X @ beta)
    return X.T @ (mu - Y)

def neg_hessian(beta):
    """Hessian of the negative log-likelihood: X.T @ diag(mu) @ X"""
    mu = np.exp(X @ beta)
    return X.T @ (mu[:, None] * X)

# Start intercept at log(sample mean) for numerical stability; other coeffs = 0
beta0 = np.r_[np.log(Y.mean()), np.zeros(X.shape[1] - 1)]

result_reg = minimize(
    neg_loglik,
    beta0,
    method="BFGS",
    jac=neg_score,
    options={"maxiter": 5000, "gtol": 1e-8}
)

beta_hat = result_reg.x

# SE = sqrt of diagonal of inverse observed information matrix (analytic Hessian)

cov_hat = inv(H)

results_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Coefficient": beta_hat,
    "Std. Error": se_hat,
    "z-value": beta_hat / se_hat
}, index=feature_names)

Interpreting the Coefficients

title: Does Blueprinty’s Software Help Engineers Win More Patents?
text:
The Effect of Blueprinty’s Software
The coefficient on iscustomer tells us the multiplicative change in the expected patent rate. But it’s more useful to report the estimated effect in patent counts — a quantity that non-technical stakeholders can immediately understand.
We use the Average Treatment Effect (ATE) via counterfactual prediction:

Create \(X_0\): set iscustomer = 0 for every firm.
Create \(X_1\): set iscustomer = 1 for every firm.
Predict \(\hat{y}_0 = \exp(X_0 \hat\beta)\) and \(\hat{y}_1 = \exp(X_1 \hat\beta)\).
Report \(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i (\hat{y}_{1i} - \hat{y}_{0i})\).



Code
beta_glm = glm_result.params.to_numpy()

cust_col = feature_names.index("iscustomer")
X0 = X.copy(); X0[:, cust_col] = 0
X1 = X.copy(); X1[:, cust_col] = 1

y_hat_0 = np.exp(X0 @ beta_glm)
y_hat_1 = np.exp(X1 @ beta_glm)

ate = np.mean(y_hat_1 - y_hat_0)
print(f"Estimated ATE: {ate:.4f} additional patents per firm over 5 years")


Estimated ATE: 0.7928 additional patents per firm over 5 years











After holding firm age and regional location constant, switching a firm from non-customer to customer status is associated with an average of 0.793 additional patents over a five-year window. Given a typical baseline of around 3–4 patents per firm, this represents a meaningful relative increase.

What This Analysis Does — and Doesn’t — Establish
This result is consistent with Blueprinty’s marketing claim, and the association survives controls for two important confounders (age and region). However, a few important caveats apply:

Observational, not experimental: firms were not randomly assigned to use Blueprinty’s software. Firms that chose to adopt it may differ from non-adopters in ways we cannot fully measure — internal R&D culture, management quality, or patent strategy.
No pre-treatment baseline: ideally we would observe each firm’s patent rate before and after adopting the software. The cross-sectional data here cannot rule out that high-patenting firms were simply more likely to become customers in the first place.
Omitted variables: the model controls for age and region but not for firm size, R&D spending, or industry sub-sector, all of which could confound the relationship.

In short: the analysis provides credible correlational evidence in support of Blueprinty’s claim, but a randomized experiment or a difference-in-differences design with panel data would be needed to make a causal claim.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MaxDiff (also called Best-Worst Scaling) is a survey methodology for measuring how strongly people prefer one item over another. On each screen of the survey, the respondent sees a small subset of items — here, four — and selects the most-preferred and least-preferred item from that subset. This process repeats over many screens, gradually building a picture of each respondent’s preference ordering across all items.
Why MaxDiff rather than a simple 1–10 rating scale? Humans are good at trade-offs — picking a best and a worst from a small set — but notoriously unreliable at calibrating numeric scales across people and time. MaxDiff forces real differentiation between items and avoids the scale-use bias that plagues Likert-style questions.
In this assignment, the 10 items are the 10 core MSBA classes at UCSD Rady. Our goal is to estimate students’ relative preference for each class using three methods of increasing sophistication:

A simple counts analysis — the percentage of times each class was picked best, minus the percentage of times it was picked worst.
The Multinomial Logit (MNL) model fit by Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), which models the underlying choice process explicitly.
The same MNL model fit by Bayesian methods via a Metropolis-Hastings MCMC sampler, which adds a prior distribution and yields full posterior uncertainty.

The three methods should broadly agree on which classes are most and least preferred. They will differ in how they quantify the strength of preference and how they communicate uncertainty — particularly in the middle of the ranking, where items are close together.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Counts Analysis
The counts analysis is the simplest summary of MaxDiff data. For each item \(j\), we compute:
\[\text{score}_j = \%\text{best}_j - \%\text{worst}_j\]
where \(\%\text{best}_j\) is the fraction of times item \(j\) was picked best out of the times it was shown, and similarly for worst. A score near +1 means the item is almost always chosen as best; near −1, almost always chosen as worst; near 0, the item is middle-of-the-pack or polarizing.


Code
item_stats = df.groupby("Item").agg(
    times_shown  = ("Response", "count"),
    times_best   = ("Response", lambda x: (x == 1).sum()),
    times_worst  = ("Response", lambda x: (x == -1).sum()),
).reset_index()

item_stats["pct_best"]  = item_stats["times_best"]  / item_stats["times_shown"]
item_stats["pct_worst"] = item_stats["times_worst"] / item_stats["times_shown"]
item_stats["score"]     = item_stats["pct_best"] - item_stats["pct_worst"]
item_stats["label"]     = item_stats["Item"].map(short_map)
item_stats = item_stats.sort_values("score", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

print(item_stats[["Item", "label", "times_shown", "times_best", "times_worst",
                   "pct_best", "pct_worst", "score"]].to_string(index=False, float_format="{:.3f}".format))

# ── Bootstrap 95% CIs on counts scores ────────────────────────────────────────

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
section: From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
text:
From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
Before fitting an MNL, we need to explain how the MaxDiff survey data converts into choice observations. The MNL model assigns each item \(j\) a utility coefficient \(\beta_j\). For a task showing items \(\{a, b, c, d\}\), the probability that item \(b\) is picked best follows the softmax:
\[P(\text{best} = b) = \frac{\exp(\beta_b)}{\exp(\beta_a) + \exp(\beta_b) + \exp(\beta_c) + \exp(\beta_d)}\]
After the best is identified, the worst is picked from the remaining 3 items — but with flipped utilities, since we are now modeling “least preferred”:
\[P(\text{worst} = c \mid \text{best} = b) = \frac{\exp(-\beta_c)}{\exp(-\beta_a) + \exp(-\beta_c) + \exp(-\beta_d)}\]
So each task yields two MNL observations: the best-pick from all 4 items, and the worst-pick from the remaining 3.
Identifiability: The softmax is invariant to adding a constant to every \(\beta\), so only \(J - 1 = 9\) of the 10 coefficients are identified. We fix \(\beta_1 = 0\) (MGTA 403 as reference) and estimate \(\beta_2, \ldots, \beta_{10}\).


Code
# ── Build task arrays (vectorized, for speed) ──────────────────────────────────
tasks_raw = []
for (rid, tid), g in df.groupby(["Record ID", "Task"]):
    items = g["Item"].values
    best  = g.loc[g["Response"] == 1, "Item"].values[0]
    worst = g.loc[g["Response"] == -1, "Item"].values[0]
    tasks_raw.append((items, best, worst))

n_tasks = len(tasks_raw)
items_mat    = np.zeros((n_tasks, 4), dtype=int)
best_arr     = np.zeros(n_tasks, dtype=int)
worst_arr    = np.zeros(n_tasks, dtype=int)
remaining_mat = np.zeros((n_tasks, 3), dtype=int)

for t, (items, best, worst) in enumerate(tasks_raw):
    idx = np.array(items) - 1
    items_mat[t]     = idx
    best_arr[t]      = best - 1
    worst_arr[t]     = worst - 1
    remaining_mat[t] = idx[idx != (best - 1)]

print(f"Total MNL observations: {n_tasks} tasks × 2 = {n_tasks*2}")


Total MNL observations: 680 tasks × 2 = 1360

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Maximum Likelihood
The log-likelihood, summing over all tasks, is:
\[\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{\text{tasks}} \Big[\, \beta_{j^*_b} - \log\!\!\sum_{j \in \text{shown}}\!\!\exp(\beta_j) - \beta_{j^*_w} - \log\!\!\!\!\!\sum_{j \in \text{shown} \setminus \{j^*_b\}}\!\!\!\!\exp(-\beta_j) \,\Big]\]
where \(j^*_b\) is the best-picked item and \(j^*_w\) is the worst-picked item in that task. We use the log-sum-exp trick throughout for numerical stability.


Code
from scipy.optimize import minimize
from scipy.linalg import inv as linv

def log_likelihood(beta):
    """beta: length-9 vector for items 2–10; item 1 fixed at 0."""
    b = np.concatenate([[0.0], beta])           # length 10
    utils_best   = b[items_mat]                 # (n_tasks, 4)
    m1 = utils_best.max(axis=1, keepdims=True)
    lse_best     = m1.squeeze() + np.log(np.sum(np.exp(utils_best - m1), axis=1))
    ll_best      = b[best_arr] - lse_best

    neg_utils_worst = -b[remaining_mat]         # (n_tasks, 3)
    m2 = neg_utils_worst.max(axis=1, keepdims=True)
    lse_worst    = m2.squeeze() + np.log(np.sum(np.exp(neg_utils_worst - m2), axis=1))
    ll_worst     = -b[worst_arr] - lse_worst

    return np.sum(ll_best) + np.sum(ll_worst)

# ── Optimize ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
res = minimize(
    lambda x: -log_likelihood(x),
    x0     = np.zeros(9),
    method = "BFGS",
    options= {"maxiter": 10000, "gtol": 1e-6}
)
beta_mle = np.concatenate([[0.0], res.x])   # length 10 (item 1 = 0)

# ── Standard errors via numerical Hessian ─────────────────────────────────────
from scipy.optimize import approx_fprime
eps = 1e-4
n   = 9
hess = np.zeros((n, n))
for i in range(n):
    x1, x2 = res.x.copy(), res.x.copy()
    x1[i] += eps; x2[i] -= eps
    g1 = approx_fprime(x1, lambda x: -log_likelihood(x), 1e-5)
    g2 = approx_fprime(x2, lambda x: -log_likelihood(x), 1e-5)
    hess[i] = (g1 - g2) / (2 * eps)
se_mle = np.concatenate([[np.nan], np.sqrt(np.diag(linv(hess)))])

# ── Shares of preference ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
shares_mle = np.exp(beta_mle) / np.exp(beta_mle).sum()

# ── Results table ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
mle_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Item"   : range(1, 11),
    "Label"  : [short_map[i] for i in range(1, 11)],
    "beta"   : beta_mle,
    "SE"     : se_mle,
    "share"  : shares_mle,
}).sort_values("share", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

print(mle_df.to_string(index=False, float_format="{:.3f}".format))

    5        Biz Analytics  0.744 0.142  0.117
    7   Customer Analytics  0.656 0.142  0.107
    4           Large Data  0.555 0.140  0.097
    8             Capstone  0.443 0.140  0.087
    2                  SQL  0.438 0.137  0.086
   10     Biz Intelligence  0.236 0.137  0.070
    1              AI Math  0.000   NaN  0.056
    3          Mkt/Fin/Ops -0.067 0.139  0.052
    6 Analytics Consulting -0.239 0.141  0.044




Code

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

               for s in mle_df["share"]]
ax.barh(range(len(mle_df)), mle_df["share"], color=colors_mle, alpha=0.85, height=0.6)

Comparison with counts: The MLE ranking is nearly identical to the counts ranking at the top and bottom. Marketing Analytics (MGTA 495) again dominates with a share of ~0.28, far above the uniform 0.10 baseline. The MLE adds precision — standard errors quantify how uncertain each estimate is — and explicitly accounts for the context in which each item appeared (which other items it competed against), which the simple counts method ignores.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Bayesian Estimation
The Bayesian approach specifies a prior over \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) and combines it with the likelihood to obtain a posterior:
\[\pi(\boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \boldsymbol{y}) \;\propto\; L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \cdot \pi(\boldsymbol{\beta})\]
We use a weakly informative prior: \(\beta_j \sim N(0, 10)\) for each of the 9 free parameters. This places 95% of prior probability in \([-6.2, 6.2]\), which is very diffuse relative to the MLE estimates.
We draw from the posterior using a random-walk Metropolis-Hastings sampler: at each step, we propose \(\boldsymbol{\beta}^* = \boldsymbol{\beta} + \epsilon\) where \(\epsilon \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 I)\), and accept with probability \(\min(1,\; e^{\log\pi(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) - \log\pi(\boldsymbol{\beta})})\).


Code
def log_prior(beta):
    return np.sum(-0.5 * beta**2 / 10.0)   # N(0, 10) on each component

def log_post(beta):
    return log_likelihood(beta) + log_prior(beta)

np.random.seed(42)
n_iter  = 20000
step = 0.08  # tuned to ~30% acceptance
K       = 9
beta    = np.zeros(K)
lp      = log_post(beta)
draws   = np.zeros((n_iter, K))
accept  = 0

for t in range(n_iter):
    prop    = beta + np.random.normal(0, step, K)
    lp_prop = log_post(prop)
    if np.log(np.random.uniform()) < lp_prop - lp:
        beta   = prop
        lp     = lp_prop
        accept += 1
    draws[t] = beta

accept_rate = accept / n_iter
print(f"Acceptance rate: {accept_rate:.3f}")


Acceptance rate: 0.293




Code
# ── Diagnostics: trace plots ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
burnin  = n_iter // 4   # discard first 25%
post    = draws[burnin:]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(10, 6), sharex=True)
param_names = [f"β_{i+2} ({short_map[i+2]})" for i in range(3)]
colors_trace = ["#1565C0", "#2E7D32", "#6A1B9A"]

for i, (ax, col) in enumerate(zip(axes, colors_trace)):
    ax.plot(draws[:, i], color=col, linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.8)
    ax.axvline(burnin, color="red", linestyle="--", linewidth=1, label="Burn-in end")
    ax.set_ylabel(param_names[i], fontsize=9)
    if i == 0:
        ax.legend(fontsize=8)

axes[-1].set_xlabel("Iteration")
fig.suptitle("Trace Plots — First 3 Parameters", fontsize=11)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The chains show the characteristic “fuzzy caterpillar” pattern after burn-in, indicating the sampler has mixed well. There is no visible drift or slow wandering.


Code
# ── Posterior summaries ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
post_mean_9 = post.mean(axis=0)
ci_lo_9     = np.percentile(post, 2.5,  axis=0)
ci_hi_9     = np.percentile(post, 97.5, axis=0)

# Full 10-vector (prepend item 1 = 0 fixed)
beta_post_full = np.column_stack([np.zeros(len(post)), post])   # (draws, 10)
shares_draws   = np.exp(beta_post_full) / np.exp(beta_post_full).sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)
shares_post    = shares_draws.mean(axis=0)
shares_ci_lo   = np.percentile(shares_draws, 2.5,  axis=0)
shares_ci_hi   = np.percentile(shares_draws, 97.5, axis=0)

post_mean_full = np.concatenate([[0.0], post_mean_9])
ci_lo_full     = np.concatenate([[np.nan], ci_lo_9])
ci_hi_full     = np.concatenate([[np.nan], ci_hi_9])

bayes_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Item"       : range(1, 11),
    "Label"      : [short_map[i] for i in range(1, 11)],
    "post_mean"  : post_mean_full,
    "ci_lo"      : ci_lo_full,
    "ci_hi"      : ci_hi_full,
    "share"      : shares_post,
    "share_lo"   : shares_ci_lo,
    "share_hi"   : shares_ci_hi,
}).sort_values("share", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

print(bayes_df[["Item","Label","post_mean","ci_lo","ci_hi","share","share_lo","share_hi"]].to_string(
    index=False, float_format="{:.3f}".format))

          bayes_df["share_hi"] - bayes_df["share"]],

)

Comparison to MLE: The posterior means closely track the MLE point estimates, as expected with 680 tasks of data — the likelihood dominates the weakly informative prior. The posterior standard deviations are very close to the MLE standard errors. The key advantage of the Bayesian approach is that credible intervals on derived quantities (like preference shares) arise naturally by pushing every MCMC draw through the softmax — no delta method required.

Discussion: The three methods agree strongly on the top and bottom of the ranking. Marketing Analytics (MGTA 495) is the clear winner under all three methods, and Analytics Consulting (MGTA 444) and AI Math (MGTA 403) consistently sit at the bottom. Disagreements arise in the middle cluster — SQL, Mkt/Fin/Ops, and Business Intelligence — where differences in utility are small enough that sampling noise can flip adjacent ranks.
The MNL models add two things beyond counts. First, they account for the competitive context of each task: every item that was shown but not picked provides information (an implicit “not this one”), which the simple count ignores. Second, they place all items on a common utility scale (the \(\beta\) coefficients) and derive shares that are interpretable as market-like probabilities.
Bayes adds one more layer over MLE: credible intervals on derived quantities like shares of preference. To get confidence intervals on shares from MLE, one would need the delta method, a non-trivial calculation. With MCMC, it is effortless — just apply the softmax to every posterior draw and take quantiles.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Discussion
Substantive finding: MSBA students most strongly prefer Marketing Analytics (MGTA 495, Yavorsky) and Customer Analytics (MGTA 455, Nijs) — both of which are elective-style, applied analytics courses with direct career relevance. Large Data (MGTA 452) also ranks highly, likely because data engineering skills are highly valued in the job market. At the other end, Analytics Consulting, Mkt/Fin/Ops, and AI Math form the lowest-ranked group, perhaps because students view them as less directly applicable or enjoyable relative to the rest of the curriculum.
Methodological takeaways:

Counts analysis is fast and transparent — a good first pass or executive summary. It requires no modeling assumptions.
MLE MNL is rigorous and provides point estimates with classical standard errors. It is the right tool when you need a single number to report or compare across groups.
Bayesian MNL shines when you need uncertainty on derived quantities (preference shares, market simulations, rank probabilities), want to propagate uncertainty through downstream decisions, or plan to extend the model to estimate individual-level preferences (hierarchical Bayes).

Caveats: The sample is self-selected MSBA students surveyed at a single point in time. Responses likely reflect where students are in the program, their career goals, and prior coursework — none of which are controlled for here. Preferences may also shift as the curriculum evolves.
Next steps: With more time, I would fit a hierarchical Bayes (HB) model to estimate individual-level \(\beta_i\) vectors, which would reveal whether there are latent preference segments (e.g., students headed to tech vs. finance vs. consulting). I would also explore whether preferences vary by cohort year or prior work experience.

title: Key Drivers Analysis
text:
Setup


Code
import math
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from itertools import combinations
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor

df = pd.read_csv("files/data_for_drivers_analysis.csv")

y_col = "satisfaction"
x_cols = ["trust", "build", "differs", "easy", "appealing",
          "rewarding", "popular", "service", "impact"]

X = df[x_cols].values
y = df[y_col].values
p = X.shape[1]

print(f"N = {len(df):,}  |  p = {p} predictors")


N = 2,553  |  p = 9 predictors

title: Key Drivers Analysis
text:
Methods

1. Pearson Correlations
The squared Pearson correlation between each predictor \(X_j\) and \(Y\), normalized to sum to 100%.
\[r_j = \text{corr}(X_j, Y)^2\]
This is equivalent to the \(R^2\) from a simple regression of \(Y\) on \(X_j\) alone. It is fast and always gives the correct sign, but “double counts” shared variance when predictors are correlated.


Code
pearson = np.array([np.corrcoef(X[:, j], y)[0, 1]**2 for j in range(p)])
pearson_pct = pearson / pearson.sum()




2. Standardized Regression Coefficients
Standardize all variables (mean 0, SD 1), run a single OLS regression, and use the squared coefficients as importance weights.
\[\hat{\beta}_{j, \text{std}}^2 \quad \text{from} \quad y_{\text{std}} = \sum_j \beta_j X_{j,\text{std}}\]
Fast (one regression), but under-counts contributions from correlated predictors because collinearity inflates standard errors and shrinks coefficients.


Code
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_std = scaler.fit_transform(X)
y_std = (y - y.mean()) / y.std()
coef = LinearRegression().fit(X_std, y_std).coef_
std_coefs_pct = coef**2 / (coef**2).sum()




3. Drop-one Usefulness (\(\Delta R^2\))
The incremental \(R^2\) lost by removing variable \(j\) from the full model:
\[\Delta R^2_j = R^2_{\text{full}} - R^2_{-j}\]
Requires \(K + 1\) regressions. Like standardized coefficients, this under-counts the contribution of correlated predictors (shared variance is not allocated).


Code
def r2(X_sub, y):
    if X_sub.ndim == 1:
        X_sub = X_sub.reshape(-1, 1)
    if X_sub.shape[1] == 0:
        return 0.0
    return LinearRegression().fit(X_sub, y).score(X_sub, y)

r2_full = r2(X, y)
usefulness = np.array([
    r2_full - r2(X[:, [k for k in range(p) if k != j]], y)
    for j in range(p)
])
usefulness_pct = usefulness / usefulness.sum()




4. Shapley Values (Exact, Hand-Coded)
Shapley values from cooperative game theory fairly distribute the total \(R^2\) across all predictors. For each variable \(j\), we compute its average marginal contribution across all \(2^{p-1}\) possible subsets \(S \subseteq \{1,\ldots,p\} \setminus \{j\}\):
\[\phi_j = \sum_{S \subseteq P \setminus \{j\}} \frac{|S|!\,(p-|S|-1)!}{p!} \left[ R^2(S \cup \{j\}) - R^2(S) \right]\]
This is the gold standard for variable importance: it uniquely satisfies efficiency (values sum to \(R^2\)), symmetry, and fairness. Computationally expensive (\(2^p\) regressions), but with \(p = 9\) this is only 512 models — easily tractable.
The code below implements the formula directly, without any Shapley library.


Code
shapley = np.zeros(p)
for j in range(p):
    others = [k for k in range(p) if k != j]
    for size in range(p):
        for subset in combinations(others, size):
            subset = list(subset)
            w = math.factorial(size) * math.factorial(p - size - 1) / math.factorial(p)
            X_with = X[:, subset + [j]]
            X_without = X[:, subset] if subset else np.empty((X.shape[0], 0))
            shapley[j] += w * (r2(X_with, y) - r2(X_without, y))

shapley_pct = shapley / shapley.sum()

# Sanity check: Shapley values should sum to the full R^2
print(f"Sum of Shapley values: {shapley.sum():.4f}")
print(f"Full model R^2:        {r2_full:.4f}")


Sum of Shapley values: 0.1090
Full model R^2:        0.1090




5. Johnson’s Relative Weights (Epsilon)
An algebraic approximation to Shapley values for linear regression (Johnson 2000). It orthogonalizes the predictors via SVD, fits OLS on the orthogonal components, then rotates the contributions back to the original predictors:
\[\epsilon_j = \sum_k \hat{\lambda}_k \cdot v_{kj}^2\]
where \(\hat{\lambda}_k\) is the variance explained by the \(k\)-th orthogonal component and \(v_{kj}\) are loadings from the SVD. Extremely fast (just matrix algebra), and a good approximation to Shapley values — but only available for linear regression.


Code
U, s, Vt = np.linalg.svd(X_std, full_matrices=False)
Z = U * s
beta_z = LinearRegression(fit_intercept=False).fit(Z, y_std).coef_
johnson = np.array([np.sum(beta_z**2 * Vt[:, j]**2) for j in range(p)])
johnson_pct = johnson / johnson.sum()




6. Random Forest: Mean Decrease in Impurity (Gini)
A Random Forest grows 500 decision trees on bootstrapped samples, each using a random feature subset. Variable importance is the average reduction in MSE each time variable \(j\) is used as a split, aggregated across all trees and normalized:
\[\text{Importance}_j = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \sum_{\text{nodes using } j} \Delta \text{MSE}\]
Model-free (no linearity assumption), captures interactions, and is computed as a byproduct of fitting the forest.


Code
rf = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=500, random_state=42, n_jobs=-1)
rf.fit(X, y)
rf_pct = rf.feature_importances_ / rf.feature_importances_.sum()

title: Key Drivers Analysis
text:
Takeaways
The methods fall into three groups by how they handle correlated predictors:
Over-counts shared variance — Pearson correlations assign full credit to each predictor independently, so totals “double count” when predictors are correlated. trust, service, and impact score highest here, but the spread across attributes is compressed.
Under-counts shared variance — Standardized coefficients and drop-one both attribute shared variance only to the predictors remaining in the model. This dramatically deflates estimates for correlated variables (build, easy, rewarding all drop near 1%) and over-weights the single strongest driver (impact jumps to ~40% on drop-one).
Fairly allocates shared variance — Shapley values and Johnson’s epsilon split credit appropriately. The three top drivers are consistent across both: impact, trust, and service, each accounting for roughly 12–22%. The Random Forest ranking — which makes no linearity assumption — agrees and identifies the same three as the most important.

Recommendation: Shapley values (or Johnson’s epsilon as a fast approximation) are preferred for key drivers analysis. The business should focus on improving impact (“makes a difference in my life”), trust, and service for the greatest gains in customer satisfaction.
